subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately and effectively with clear and appropriate focus throughout.
Research question is clearly stated, focused, and consistently connected to the analysis.
Range of secondary sources is relevant and generally well chosen, demonstrating informed selection.
weaknesses: Occasional verbosity and minor digressions slightly obscure focus.
Methodology description lacks detail on data-selection criteria and calculation procedures.
Research question could be introduced earlier to streamline the narrative.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place two dimensions in the top band (5â6) and one in the 3â4 band. Communication of topic and RQ are strong (each assessed at 5), while methodology completeness sits at the top of band 3â4 (assessed at 4). Averaging these and accounting for minor digressions yields a final score of 5, reflecting high performance with room for improvement in methodological detail.

strengths: Knowledge of economic theory is clear and coherent, with effective use of sources (e.g., Ministry of Health data, elasticity studies).
Terminology (negative externality, hyperbolic discounting) is generally accurate and applied consistently.
Source material is mostly relevant and appropriately applied to support arguments.
weaknesses: Application of one major graph lacks methodological explanation, reducing integration consistency.
Some conceptual inaccuracies appear (confusion between MPC and MSC).
Linkages for subgroup (ethnic) analysis are descriptive and need more specific data support.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Most strands fall in the 3â4 band (application 3; knowledge 4; terminology 4). The student demonstrates solid understanding overall, with minor conceptual and integration issues. Scaling these to a 6-point criterion and rewarding the consistency of source use supports a final mark of 5.

strengths: Research draws predominantly on appropriate and relevant sources directly linked to the research question.
Clear analysis links data trends to economic theory, using diagrams to illustrate welfare impacts.
Argumentation is logical and structured, with evaluation of alternative explanations and awareness of data limitations.
weaknesses: Quantitative analysis lacks depth (no regression or elasticity recalculation).
Discussion sometimes rests on correlation rather than causation, limiting robustness.
Critical evaluation of policy subsections (e.g., Amendment Act) remains brief and speculative.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fall into the 4â6 band (research quality ~5; analysis ~4; evaluation ~4). The work consistently provides reasoned analysis and relevant research but lacks sustained quantitative depth. A holistic placement in the mid-to-upper range of the 4â6 band corresponds to a score of 9 out of 12, reflecting solid critical thinking with areas for deeper evaluation.

strengths: Clearly appropriate structure following all IB EE conventions (abstract-style introduction, section headings, TOC, conclusion).
Layout and formatting (numbered pages, labeled figures, consistent bibliography) are correctly applied throughout.
Logical progression of ideas enhances readability.
weaknesses: Excessively long table of contents with dense leaders impedes readability.
Minor formatting glitches in tables and superscripts detract slightly from polish.
Some figure labels (axes titles, units) could be clearer.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation strands both achieved top-band assessments (4/4). Despite minor formatting and labeling issues, the structure and layout fully meet rubric expectations, justifying the maximum score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated in bold on page 1 and is narrow (NZ market, 2019-2024) and directly linked to every analytical section. Some sub-sections drift into general policy discussion, but the investigation continues to circle back to the guiding question, satisfying the âclearly stated, focused and effectively connectedâ descriptor. Minor breadth in the âalternative eventsâ subsection justifies a 5 rather than full 6.

The student communicates the topicâCovid-19âs impact on cigarette consumption in New Zealandâclearly in both the title and introduction. The purpose (to measure the pandemicâs effect on demand and resulting externalities) remains visible throughout the body sections and is appropriate for systematic economic investigation relying on secondary data. Occasional verbosity does not obscure focus, so overall communication meets the descriptors of the top band, though minor digressions warrant a score of 5 rather than 6.

Most figures, statistics and quotations stem from credible sources and are generally employed to support arguments (e.g., Ministry of Health smoking rates, elasticity estimates). Yet replication of numbers is sometimes descriptive, and one major graph is a simple forecast without methodological explanation. Because application is âmostly relevantâ but not consistently integrated, the strand sits mid-band with 3 marks.

The essay follows IB EE conventions: abstract-like introduction, clear section headings, numbered figures, TOC and conclusion. Arguments progress logically, and required elements are present. Minor repetition and an over-long TOC cell are stylistic rather than structural flaws, justifying full marks in this strand.

Title page lists word count; pages appear numbered; diagrams labelled; bibliography in consistent style. Occasional formatting glitches (run-on table, superscript tags) are minor, so layout considerations are âpresent and correctly applied,â meriting 4.

The student provides a reasoned argument attributing decreased smoking to Covid-19 and evaluates alternative explanations (tax, plain packaging). Evaluation recognises data limitations, but critical appraisal of methodology and counter-evidence is brief. Logical structure exists but lacks sustained critical depth, positioning the strand mid-band at 4 marks.

Analysis links data trends to theoryâlockdown-induced demand shifts, health-risk perception affecting MPBâ and uses diagrams to illustrate welfare impacts. Nevertheless, quantitative depth is limited: no regression, elasticity recalculation or rigorous cost-benefit quantification. Several causal claims rest on correlation. Thus analysis is âclear with mostly supported conclusions,â warranting a 4.

The essay demonstrates clear knowledge of negative externalities, elasticity, dead-weight loss and public-health policy, applying them coherently to the NZ context. Occasional misstatements (confusing MPC and MSC lines) and superficial treatment of ethnic differences prevent the work from reaching the highest band, but overall understanding is solid and sources are used partially effectively, meriting 4.

The majority of cited research (national surveys, peer-reviewed studies) is directly relevant to the RQ. Some peripheral sources (e.g., UNDP general socioeconomic impact) offer limited added value, but on balance appropriateness is clear, situating the work comfortably in the 4-6 band at 5 marks.

A reasonable range of secondary sources is cited (government surveys, academic journals, OECD, UNDP etc.) with brief comment on why they were chosen and acknowledgement of limitations when extrapolating externality costs. However, the methodology section is short, gives scant detail on data-selection criteria and contains only one paragraph on the calculation approach. Evidence of âeffective and informedâ selection is partial rather than âclearâ, placing it in the 3-4 band at the top of that range.

Economic terminologyânegative externality, marginal propensity, hyperbolic discountingâis generally accurate and applied consistently. Notation in diagrams occasionally lapses (e.g., labeling MPC = MSC) but these are minor. Consistency and correctness fit the upper end of the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The welfare loss diagram is well-labeled and supports the discussion of deadweight loss effectively.
The inclusion of ethnic group analysis demonstrates relevance, but the rationale linking group behavior to consumption should be supported by specific data or citations.
The title is precise and aligns effectively with the research focus, guiding the reader immediately to the investigationâs scope between 2019 and 2024 in NZâs cigarette market.
Providing the word count at the start meets IB formatting requirements and demonstrates attention to administrative detail.
The table of contents includes all required sections, but excessive dotted leaders impede readability; consider reducing line lengths or using page tabs.
Page-number alignment appears slightly inconsistent in the footer; ensure uniform placement and formatting across all pages.
The introduction provides comprehensive background, but the explicit research question appears late; introduce it earlier to strengthen focus.
Footnotes exhibit inconsistent formatting with literal HTML remnants (<sup>); refine citation style to maintain presentation standards.
There is confusion between private and social cost in the MPC/MSC description; ensure that MPC and MSC are clearly distinguished to avoid conceptual inaccuracies.
Post-pandemic data for 2024 provides current context, and highlighting the continuation of trends is a clear strength.
When introducing Figure 3, explicitly reference axes labels and units to improve clarity for the reader.
Figure 3âs forecast vs. actual comparison is insightful, but including confidence intervals around the forecast would enhance robustness.
The 4.7% pandemic effect is clearly stated, yet the calculation or data derivation should be shown in an appendix for transparency.
The methodology header is present, but the description lacks detail on dataâselection criteria; expand on sampling frames and inclusion thresholds.
The cost externality estimation method is justified by comparison but would benefit from an explanation of statistical assumptions underlying the GDP extrapolation.
Quantitative trend calculation (0.4125% annual decline) is clear, though the formula should be shown for reproducibility.
Subsection header formatting (â1.2 COVID-19â) is consistent, but the narrative conflates âmore cigarettesâ with overall consumption decline; clarify this apparent contradiction.
Use of multiple credible sources strengthens the argument, yet integrating a regression or elasticity estimate could deepen quantitative analysis.
Lockdown effects are logically argued, yet the potential counter-effect of increased home consumption is insufficiently explored; consider empirical survey findings.
The inelastic demand discussion appropriately applies economic theory, reflecting a sound understanding of habit-forming goods in crisis contexts.
Claim that MÄori consumption decreased less requires supportive data; include group-specific elasticity or percentage changes for credibility.
The supplyâdemand shift diagram is described correctly, but ensure consistency in notation (Pâ, Pâ) and label shifts directly on the graph.
Analysis of plain packaging uses nudge theory effectively, but could be strengthened by citing an empirical evaluation from comparable jurisdictions.
The section on the 2023 Amendment Act offers insightful policy context but over-relies on speculative impacts; balance with observed preliminary data or expert commentary.
Quantifying the revenue drop ($937.5 m) is compelling, but clarify whether this figure isolates cigarette sales or covers the broader tobacco sector.
Hyperbolic discounting reference enriches the discussion of stress-induced smoking; consider defining it formally with a citation for clarity.
Figure 5 effectively illustrates the MPB shift but would benefit from clearer axis titles and an indication of magnitude for the shift.
Cost projection between $2.14 bn and $2.73 bn is well calculated, but discuss sensitivity to changes in GDP growth assumptions.
The conclusion reiterates findings effectively, yet summarising key quantitative results in bullet form would enhance clarity and reader recall.
